title: Presenting Your Stack
intro: You've chosen your stack: Next.js, Supabase, and TypeScript. Think of your stack like a band — each instrument plays a different role, and together they make music. Now a senior engineer asks you to justify your choices. Can you explain why each piece fits?

teach.title: Know Your 'Why' for Every Tool
teach.body: Anyone can list technologies. What separates good engineers is being able to explain why they chose each one. For every tool in your stack, you should be able to say: what problem it solves, what alternatives you considered, and why this option won the tradeoff.

teach.title: A Band, Not a Solo Act
teach.body: Your tech stack is like a band. Next.js is the lead vocalist — it handles the parts the audience sees. Supabase is the drummer — keeping the rhythm of your data steady in the background. TypeScript is the sound engineer — making sure nothing goes off-key. Vercel is the stage crew — setting everything up so the show goes live without a hitch. Each member has a role, and together they make music.

teach.title: Our Stack at a Glance
teach.code:
Frontend:  Next.js (React framework)
Backend:   Supabase (PostgreSQL + Auth + APIs)
Language:  TypeScript (type-safe JavaScript)
Deploy:    Vercel (optimized for Next.js)
teach.annotation: Each piece was chosen because it solves a specific need for our task manager and works well with the others. That's what a good stack looks like — tools that complement each other.

teach.body: You now have the same tech stack that startups use to build apps generating real revenue. The difference between you and them? They started. You're about to.

A good tech stack isn't about picking the 'best' tools — it's about picking tools that work well together, match your project's needs, and let you move fast. Always be ready to explain your reasoning.

start.narrative: A senior engineer reviews your project plan and says: 'Why did you pick Next.js instead of plain React with Vite? Seems like extra complexity.' How do you respond?

choices.text: "Next.js gives us file-based routing, API routes, and server-side rendering out of the box. For a full-stack task manager, that saves us from configuring a separate router, server, and build pipeline."
"Everyone uses Next.js these days, so it must be the best choice."
"The tutorial I followed used it, so I just went with that."

good-nextjs.narrative: The senior nods. 'Fair points. Now defend the database. Why Supabase with PostgreSQL instead of Firebase?'

choices.text: "Tasks are structured and relational — they belong to users and have statuses. SQL handles that cleanly. Supabase gives us managed PostgreSQL, auth, and RLS in one product."
"Supabase is free, so it saves money."

bad-popularity.narrative: The senior frowns. 'Popularity isn't a technical argument. Half the internet ran on jQuery once. Try again — give me a real reason.'

choices.text: "You're right. The real reason is that Next.js bundles routing, API endpoints, and SSR into one framework, which means less setup for a full-stack app."
"Well, it has the most GitHub stars, so it's clearly the best."

weak-tutorial.narrative: The senior looks concerned. 'Following a tutorial is fine for learning, but you should understand why those choices were made. What specific Next.js features does your project actually use?'

choices.text: "File-based routing maps to our pages, and API routes let us keep backend logic in the same project without a separate server."
"I'm not sure, but it seems to work fine."

typescript-q.narrative: 'Next question. Why TypeScript instead of plain JavaScript?'

choices.text: "TypeScript catches whole categories of bugs at compile time — typos in field names, wrong argument shapes, missing properties. With an AI co-pilot writing a lot of the code, the type checker is the safety net that says 'this won't actually run' before I waste time on it."

deploy-q.narrative: 'Where does this thing actually run? Why Vercel and not, say, AWS or Render?'

choices.text: "Vercel makes Next.js — they have first-party support for everything from edge functions to image optimization. I get push-to-deploy, preview URLs per pull request, and automatic SSL with no config."
"Vercel is the easiest to deploy to."
"It was the default in the Next.js docs."

tradeoff-q.narrative: 'Last one. What do you give up by choosing this stack? Every choice has a cost — name one.'

choices.text: "Vendor coupling. Vercel pricing scales with traffic, and Supabase ties us to PostgreSQL. If we ever needed to leave, we'd swap two providers, not one — but the speed we gain in months 0-12 is worth it."
"Nothing — this stack is great in every way."

great-end.narrative: The senior smiles. 'You clearly thought this through. Stack makes sense, you can defend each piece, and you understand the tradeoffs. That's exactly the kind of thinking we look for. Ship it.'
great-end.feedback: This is how professionals evaluate technology — not by hype, but by fit, and not by 'best' but by 'best for this project, knowing what we're trading away.' Being able to name your tradeoffs is the mark of an engineer who actually understands their stack.

okay-end.narrative: The senior nods slowly. 'You can defend most of your choices, but every stack has tradeoffs. If you can't name them, you don't fully understand what you picked. Read up on the costs and you'll be in good shape.'
okay-end.feedback: You made reasonable choices but missed the tradeoff question. Every tool has costs — vendor lock-in, complexity, learning curve, scaling limits. Knowing the costs makes you ready for the day they actually hit.

weak-end.narrative: The senior sighs. 'Look, every tool in your stack should be there for a reason you can articulate, and every choice should come with a tradeoff you can name. Go research, then come back.'
weak-end.feedback: Hype is not a technical argument and 'I dunno' is not a tradeoff. Before picking any tool, write one sentence on what it does for your project, one on what you considered instead, and one on what you give up by choosing it.
